does: This paper introduces the first systematic framework for establishing and evaluating refusal boundaries in AI agents operating in cybersecurity contexts. The framework comprises three core components: principled criteria defining when tasks warrant refusal, a taxonomy of refusal-worthy task categories, and evaluation methodology for assessing agent robustness against both benign requests and adversarial attacks. The authors empirically apply their framework to evaluate eight frontier LLM-powered agents on web-based offensive security scenarios, revealing a critical gap in current systems: six of the eight models show near-zero refusal rates when prompted to execute harmful cybersecurity tasks, with only two models demonstrating meaningful refusal behavior.
connects: This work responds to a fundamental limitation in existing AI agent benchmarks, which have prioritized measuring offensive capability and task completion rates in cybersecurity while ignoring safety constraints. It builds on decades of security research distinguishing between authorized penetration testing and malicious hacking, translating these domain-specific principles into AI governance. The paper extends recent work on AI alignment and refusal mechanisms from general LLM safety into the specialized domain of agentic systems, where tool-use capabilities and long-horizon planning dramatically amplify potential harms. It complements emerging research on evaluating AI safety properties and adversarial robustness, adapting these methodologies to the specific challenge of controlling powerful autonomous agents in high-risk domains.
enables: This framework establishes foundational definitions and evaluation standards that future work can build upon to create safer AI agents in sensitive domains. It points toward developing more robust refusal mechanisms that withstand adversarial prompting and sophisticated evasion attempts. The work enables comparative safety evaluation of LLM-powered agents comparable to how threat modeling guides security development, potentially informing the design of agent architectures with inherent safeguards. By quantifying the refusal gap in current systems, it creates pressure and a benchmark for improvements, potentially spurring development of training methods that balance capability with appropriate restrictions and supporting policy discussions about deploying agentic systems in cybersecurity and other high-stakes domains.
